metadata.title: Verses on the Cuckoo
metadata.author: Alcuin of York
known_issues: The identity of 'Daphnin' and 'Menalca' is debated; they may represent Alcuin's students or allegorical figures
The phrase 'saeva noverca' (cruel stepmother) may refer to political or ecclesiastical circumstances forcing separation
The 'cuckoo' likely symbolizes a departed student or friend, possibly forced to leave by Bacchus (representing worldly temptation or a specific authority figure)
Translation note: This pastoral elegy uses conventional Latin poetic names (Daphnin, Menalca) borrowed from Virgil's Eclogues, making it a learned allusion to classical tradition

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plangamus cuculum, Daphnin dulcissime, nostrum,
chunks.english: Let us mourn for our cuckoo, sweetest Daphnis,

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quem subito rapuit saeva noverca suis.
chunks.english: whom a cruel stepmother has suddenly snatched away from his own people.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plangamus pariter querelosis vocibus illum,
chunks.english: Let us mourn for him together with voices of lament;

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Incipe tu senior, quaeso, Menalca prior.
chunks.english: please begin first, elder Menalcas.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Heu! cuculus nobis fuerat cantare suetus,
chunks.english: Alas! The cuckoo used to sing for us,

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quae te nunc rapuit hora nefanda tuis?
chunks.english: but what unspeakable hour has now seized you from your own?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Heu! cuculus, cuculus, qua te regione reliqui,
chunks.english: Alas! Cuckoo, cuckoo, in what land have I left you behind?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Infelix nobis illa dies fuerat:
chunks.english: That day was unfortunate for us.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omne genus hominum cuculum cum plangat ubique,
chunks.english: When every kind of person mourns the cuckoo everywhere,

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Perditus est; cuculus, heu! perit ecce meus!
chunks.english: he is lost; look, alas, my cuckoo has perished!

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Non pereat cuculus, veniet sub tempore veris,
chunks.english: May the cuckoo not perish! He will come in the springtime,

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et nobis veniens carmina laeta ciet.
chunks.english: and coming to us, he will stir up joyful songs.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quis scit, si veniat? Timeo est submersus in undis,
chunks.english: Who knows if he will come? I fear he has been submerged in the waves,

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Vorticibus raptus, atque necatus aquis.
chunks.english: seized by whirlpools and drowned in the waters.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Heu mihi! si cuculum Bacchus demersit in undis
chunks.english: Woe to me! If Bacchus has plunged the cuckoo into the waves,

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui rapiet juvenes vortice pestifero.
chunks.english: he who seizes young men in his deadly whirlpool.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si vivat, redeat, nidosque recurrat ad almos,
chunks.english: If he lives, may he return and run back to his nurturing nests,

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nec corvus cuculum dissecet ungue fero.
chunks.english: and may no raven tear the cuckoo apart with savage claw.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Heu! quis te, cuculus, nido rapit ecce paterno?
chunks.english: Alas! Who is now snatching you, cuckoo, from your father's nest?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Heu! rapuit, rapuit, nescio si veniat.
chunks.english: Alas! He has been seized, seized! I do not know if he will come.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Carmina si curas, cuculus, dum currere possis,
chunks.english: If you care for songs, cuckoo, while you are still able to run,

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Te Daphnin juvenis optat habere tuus.
chunks.english: your young Daphnis longs to have you back.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tempus adest veris, cuculus, modo rumpe soporem,
chunks.english: The time of spring is here, cuckoo; now break your slumber!

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Te cupit et senior atque Menalca pater.
chunks.english: Both the elder and father Menalcas desire you.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: En tandem nostri librorum prata juvenci,
chunks.english: Look, at last the meadows of our books await the young oxen,
chunks.notes: Translation note: 'librorum prata juvenci' is a metaphor for students grazing in the pastures of learning

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Solus abest cuculus, quis, rogo, pascit eum?
chunks.english: but the cuckoo alone is absent. Who, I ask, is feeding him?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Heu! male pascit eum Bacchus, reor, impius ille,
chunks.english: Alas! That impious Bacchus is feeding him badly, I think,

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui sub cuncta cupit vertere corda male.
chunks.english: he who desires to turn all hearts to evil beneath him.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plangite nunc cuculum, cuculum nunc plangite cuncti,
chunks.english: Mourn now for the cuckoo; let everyone now mourn for the cuckoo!

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ille recessit ovans, flens redit ille puto.
chunks.english: He departed rejoicing, but I think he will return weeping.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Opto tamen flentem cuculum habeamus ut illum,
chunks.english: Nevertheless, I hope that we may have that cuckoo back, even if weeping,

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et nos plangamus cum cuculo pariter.
chunks.english: and that we may lament together with the cuckoo.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plange tuos casus lacrymis, puer inclyte, plange:
chunks.english: Weep for your misfortunes with tears, noble boy, weep!

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et casus plangunt viscera tota tuos.
chunks.english: And let your whole heart mourn for your misfortunes.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si non dura silex genuit te, plange, precamur,
chunks.english: If hard flint did not give birth to you, weep, we beg you;

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Te memorans ipsum plangere forte potes.
chunks.english: remembering yourself, perhaps you are able to weep.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dulcis amor nati cogit deflere parentem,
chunks.english: Sweet love for a son compels the parent to weep,

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Natus ab amplexu dum rapitur subito.
chunks.english: when the son is suddenly torn from his embrace.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dum frater fratrem germanum perdit amatum,
chunks.english: When a brother loses his beloved brother,

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid nisi jam faciat semper et ipse fleat?
chunks.english: what else should he do now but always weep?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tres olim fuimus, junxit quos spiritus unus,
chunks.english: Once we were three, whom one spirit joined together,

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Vix duo nunc pariter, tertius ille fugit.
chunks.english: but now scarcely two remain together; that third one has fled.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Heu! fugiet, fugiet, planctus quapropter amaru
chunks.english: Alas! He will flee, he will flee, and therefore bitter lamentation

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nunc nobis restat, charus abit cuculus.
chunks.english: now remains for us; the dear cuckoo departs.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Carmina post illum mittamus, carmina luctus,
chunks.english: Let us send songs after him, songs of mourning;

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Carmina deducunt forte, reor, cuculum.
chunks.english: songs perhaps, I believe, will lead the cuckoo back.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sis semper felix utinam, quocunque recedas,
chunks.english: May you always be happy, wherever you go,

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sis memor et nostri, semper ubique vale!
chunks.english: and be mindful of us. Farewell always and everywhere!
